section.title: Overcoming the World
section.sectionRationale: As the letter closes, John binds faith, love, and obedience together and shows that faith in Jesus as the Son of God is the God given victory over the world. He then marshals the witnesses God has provided, the Spirit, the water, and the blood, and God's own testimony, all converging on the Son in whom eternal life is found.
section.thesis: Everyone born of God overcomes the world through faith in Jesus the Son of God, and this faith rests not on feeling but on the testimony God has given about His Son, whose sum is that God has granted us eternal life, and this life is in the Son alone.
section.frameNote: Three beats: faith in the Son as the victory that overcomes the world; the threefold testimony of the Spirit, the water, and the blood; and God's greater testimony, that eternal life is given in His Son.

verses.text: Everyone who believes that Jesus is the Christ has been {{gennao|born of God}}, and everyone who loves the Father also loves those born of Him.
By this we know that we love the children of God: when we love God and keep His commandments.
For this is the love of God, that we keep His commandments. And His commandments are not burdensome,
because everyone born of God {{nikao|overcomes}} the world. And this is the victory that has overcome the world: our {{pistis|faith}}.
Who then overcomes the {{kosmos|world}}? Only he who believes that Jesus is the Son of God.

units.label: The threefold testimony
units.note: the Spirit, the water, and the blood

verses.text: This is the One who came by water and blood, Jesus Christ, not by water alone, but by water and blood. And it is the Spirit who testifies to this, because the Spirit is the truth.
For there are three that testify:
the Spirit, the water, and the blood; and these three are in agreement.

units.label: God's testimony about His Son
units.note: eternal life is in the Son

verses.text: Even if we accept human testimony, the testimony of God is greater. For this is the {{martyria|testimony}} that God has given about His Son.

Whoever believes in the Son of God has this testimony within him; whoever does not believe God has made Him out to be a liar, because he has not believed in the testimony that God has given about His Son.
And this is that testimony: God has given us {{zoe|eternal life}}, and this life is in His Son.
Whoever has the Son has life; whoever does not have the Son of God does not have life.

gennao.gloss: To beget, to give birth; in the passive, to be born. This is John's word for the new birth: everyone who believes that Jesus is the Christ has been born of God. This divine begetting, not human effort, is the source of faith, of love for God and His children, and of the power to overcome the world.

nikao.gloss: To conquer, overcome, prevail, be victorious. The one born of God overcomes the world, and the decisive victory has already been won. It is the same word Revelation uses for the overcomer; here the conquest is not by force but by faith joined to the Son who has Himself overcome the world.

pistis.gloss: Faith, trust, belief; reliance on God and on His testimony about His Son. This is the victory that has overcome the world: our faith. Faith is not a work that earns the victory but the empty hand that lays hold of the victorious Christ, resting on what God has said and done.

kosmos.gloss: The world; here the created order in its organised rebellion against God, the realm that lies under the power of the evil one. To overcome the world is not to escape it but to refuse its allegiance and its lies, holding fast to Christ who has conquered it.

martyria.gloss: Testimony, witness, evidence given. John stacks up witnesses to the Son: the Spirit, the water, and the blood, and above all the testimony of God Himself. Faith rests on this reliable testimony that God has given about His Son, and the believer carries that testimony within.

zoe.gloss: Life, and especially eternal life, the very life of God shared with His people. This life is not merely endless existence but life in the Son: whoever has the Son has life. It is God's own gift, given now and secured forever in Christ.

basic.context: John is drawing his letter to a close by tying its threads together. To believe that Jesus is the Christ is to be born of God, and those born of God love the Father and His children and keep His commands, which are not a heavy burden. Why not? Because everyone born of God overcomes the world, and the victory that overcomes it is our faith. Then John points to the witnesses that make faith reasonable: Jesus came by water and blood, the Spirit testifies, and God Himself testifies about His Son. The bottom line is simple: God has given us eternal life, and this life is in His Son. Whoever has the Son has life; whoever does not have the Son does not have life.
basic.hermeneutics: This passage is often misread as if faith were a burst of willpower that conquers our troubles, or as if the water and the blood were riddles. John's point is steadier. Faith overcomes the world because it unites us to the Son who has already overcome it. The water and the blood recall how Jesus came, through His baptism and His atoning death, real events that God, the Spirit, and history attest. Assurance is not wishful thinking; it rests on God's own testimony about His Son.
basic.originalLanguages: Everyone born of God, gennao, overcomes, nikao, the world, kosmos, and the victory is our faith, pistis. God stacks up witnesses, and the key word is testimony, martyria, given supremely by God about His Son. The sum of that testimony is life, zoe, eternal life, which is found in the Son alone.
basic.typology: The threefold witness echoes the law's requirement that a matter be established by two or three witnesses; here the Spirit, the water, and the blood confirm the Son. The water and the blood also recall the cleansing and the sacrifice of the old covenant, both fulfilled in Jesus, who came by water and blood.
basic.christ: Everything centres on the Son. To believe that Jesus is the Christ and the Son of God is to be born of God and to overcome the world. He is the One who came by water and blood, attested by the Spirit. And He is the place where eternal life is found: whoever has the Son has life. There is no life in general on offer, only life in Him.
basic.god: God is the chief witness. He has not left faith to guess; He has given testimony about His Son, and to refuse it is to call God a liar. This God gives eternal life as a gift, not a wage, and He secures that life in His Son so that those who believe may hold it with confidence.
basic.crossReferences: John 16:33 says Jesus has overcome the world. John 19:34 records water and blood flowing from His pierced side. John 5 speaks of the Father's testimony about the Son. And John 20:31 states the purpose of belief: that believing, you may have life in His name.
basic.oneStory: The whole Bible calls for witnesses to the truth and points to the One in whom life is found. Here the witnesses converge on Jesus: the Spirit, the water, the blood, and God Himself. The long search for life, lost in Eden, ends in the Son, for God has given us eternal life, and this life is in His Son.

context.literary: The closing movement of the letter gathers its great themes: new birth, faith, love, obedience, and the Son. It moves from the inward evidence, faith working through love and obedience, to the outward evidence, the witnesses God has given, and lands on the gift those witnesses secure: eternal life in the Son.
context.historical: John writes against teachers who split the man Jesus from the divine Christ and denied that the Christ truly suffered. His insistence that Jesus came not by water alone but by water and blood confronts that error head on: the Son of God was truly baptised and truly died, and the Spirit, the water, and the blood agree.
context.canonical: The passage joins the Bible's witness that saving faith rests on God's own testimony, and that eternal life is located in the Son, not in religion in general. It stands with John's Gospel, written that we may believe and have life, and with the whole canon's call to receive the witness God has given.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To assure believers that faith in Jesus as the Christ and Son of God is the God given victory over the world, resting not on feeling but on the witnesses God has provided, and to press home that eternal life is found in the Son and nowhere else.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage describes what is true of everyone born of God, that they overcome the world by faith, and it declares God's testimony about His Son. Woven through is the summons to believe that testimony and to keep the commandments that flow from love, which John says are not burdensome.

debates.question: What are the water and the blood in verse 6?

views.label: Jesus' baptism and His atoning death
views.case: The strongest reading takes the water as Jesus' baptism, where the Father and the Spirit attested Him, and the blood as His death on the cross. John stresses 'not by water alone, but by water and blood' to refute those who said the divine Christ came upon Jesus at His baptism but left before He died. The Son of God was truly baptised and truly crucified, and the Spirit testifies to both. This fits the letter's battle for a Christ who really came in the flesh and really suffered.

views.label: The two sacraments, baptism and the Lord's Supper
views.case: Some read water as baptism and blood as the Lord's Supper; but the framing points to events in Jesus' own coming, not to later church ordinances.

views.label: The water and blood from His pierced side
views.case: Others point to John 19:34, the water and blood that flowed at the cross; a true echo, but John here speaks of how Jesus came, spanning His baptism and His death, not only the moment of the spear.

debates.bearing: The strongest reading is that Jesus came by water and blood, His baptism and His atoning death, both attested by the Spirit. This guards the full truth of the incarnation and the cross against every attempt to divide the man Jesus from the divine Son, and it anchors faith in real events God has witnessed.

debates.question: In what sense does our faith overcome the world?

views.label: Faith overcomes by uniting us to the victorious Christ
views.case: The strongest reading is that the victory is Christ's, and faith is the means by which His conquest becomes ours. John says the victory has already overcome the world, and then names our faith; faith is not a separate force but the hand that grasps the Son who has overcome, John 16:33. This keeps the glory on Christ and gives the believer real, present assurance.

views.label: Faith is our ongoing moral triumph over temptation
views.case: Some make the victory chiefly our daily conquest of worldly desire; there is truth in the fruit, but the ground of victory is the finished work of Christ received by faith, not our striving.

views.label: Faith secures worldly success and ease
views.case: Others twist it into a promise of prosperity or freedom from hardship; but the world John speaks of is the realm in rebellion against God, and the victory is holding to Christ against it, not gaining its rewards.

debates.bearing: The strongest orthodox reading is that our faith overcomes the world because it unites us to the Son who has already overcome it. The victory is His, received by trust, so assurance rests on Christ's finished work rather than on the strength of our own resolve.

study.originalLanguages: John gathers his letter with a chain of key words. To be born of God, gennao, is to receive a new life that believes, loves, and obeys, and everyone so born overcomes, nikao, the world, kosmos, the ordered rebellion that lies under the evil one. The weapon is not willpower but pistis, faith, the trust that lays hold of the Son who has already conquered. Then John turns to evidence, martyria, testimony: the Spirit, the water, and the blood agree, and above them all stands the testimony of God Himself about His Son. The sum of that witness is zoe, life, eternal life, and John locates it with precision: this life is in His Son, and whoever has the Son has life.

typology.type: The requirement of two or three witnesses
typology.fulfillment: The law that established a matter by multiple witnesses, Deuteronomy 19:15, answered as the Spirit, the water, and the blood together testify to the Son.

typology.type: Water for cleansing and blood for sacrifice
typology.fulfillment: The washings and offerings of the old covenant fulfilled in Jesus, who came by water and blood and cleanses and atones once for all.

study.christ: The Son is the whole point of the passage. Faith in Him as the Christ and the Son of God is birth from God and victory over the world, for He has overcome it. He came by water and blood, truly baptised and truly slain, and the Spirit testifies that this is so. Above all, He is where life is: God has given us eternal life, and this life is in His Son, so that to have the Son is to have life and to lack Him is to lack it.
study.god: God is the great witness who does not leave faith to guesswork. He has testified about His Son, and to reject that testimony is to make God a liar. He is the giver of eternal life, granting it freely and lodging it securely in His Son. The God who is light and love here shows Himself faithful and true, worthy of the trust that overcomes the world.

crossReferences.note: Take heart; I have overcome the world, the victory faith lays hold of.
Water and blood flowed from Jesus' pierced side, a witness to His real death.
These are written that you may believe and have life in His name, matching life found in the Son.
A matter is established by two or three witnesses, the pattern behind the threefold testimony.

study.oneStory: From the beginning God has borne witness to the truth and promised life to those who trust Him. Here the witnesses gather around Jesus: the Spirit, the water, the blood, and God Himself. The life lost in Eden and longed for through the ages is found at last in the Son, for God has given us eternal life, and this life is in His Son. Faith in Him is the victory that overcomes the rebel world.

sermon.title: Faith Is the Victory
sermon.why: Begg preaches this section, 1 John 5:1-5, showing that genuine faith in Jesus as the Son of God is the victory that overcomes the world and the ground of assurance. A clear, orthodox handling of the passage.

quiz.q: According to verse 4, what is the victory that overcomes the world?

options.text: Our faith, which lays hold of Christ who has already overcome the world.
options.note: The victory is Christ's, and faith is the hand that receives it.

options.text: Our steady moral effort against temptation.
options.note: The fruit is real, but the ground of victory is faith in the victorious Christ, not our striving.

options.text: A promise of worldly success and ease.
options.note: The world here is the realm in rebellion against God; the victory is holding to Christ against it.

options.text: Nothing; the world cannot be overcome.
options.note: John says the opposite: everyone born of God overcomes the world.

quiz.q: What does John mean that Jesus came 'by water and blood' (verse 6)?

options.text: By His baptism and His atoning death, both real and attested by the Spirit.
options.note: John stresses not water alone but water and blood, against those who denied that the Son truly suffered.

options.text: By the water and blood that flowed from His pierced side.
options.note: A true echo of John 19:34, but John speaks of how Jesus came, spanning His baptism and His death.

options.text: By the sacraments of baptism and the Lord's Supper.
options.note: The framing points to events in Jesus' own coming, not to later church ordinances.

options.text: By a symbolic Christ who never truly suffered.
options.note: This is the very error John writes to refute.

quiz.q: Whose testimony does John say is greatest?

options.text: God's own testimony about His Son.
options.note: The testimony of God is greater, and faith rests on it.

options.text: The witness of reliable human teachers.
options.note: John accepts human testimony but says the testimony of God is greater still.

options.text: The believer's inner feelings.
options.note: Feelings rise and fall; John grounds assurance in God's testimony, though believers do carry it within.

options.text: No testimony can be trusted.
options.note: John says God's testimony is sure, and to reject it is to call God a liar.

quiz.q: How does John describe eternal life in verses 11 and 12?

options.text: As God's gift, located in His Son, so that to have the Son is to have life.
options.note: God has given us eternal life, and this life is in His Son.

options.text: As God's gift that we then keep by our own faithfulness.
options.note: Life is a gift secured in the Son, not maintained by our performance.

options.text: As a reward earned by obedience.
options.note: John calls it a gift God has given, not a wage we earn.

options.text: As something every person already possesses.
options.note: John says whoever does not have the Son does not have life.

options.text: Because those born of God overcome the world, and love makes His commands the glad response of a new heart.
options.note: New birth and love, not grudging duty, make obedience light.

options.text: Because with practice and discipline obedience grows easier over time.
options.note: Growth is real, but John roots this in the new birth and love, not mere self-discipline.

options.text: Because God has relaxed what He requires of believers.
options.note: God has not lowered His commands; He has given a new heart that loves to keep them.

options.text: Because keeping the commands is what earns our new birth.
options.note: John reverses the order: new birth comes first, and obedience flows from it.

questions: John says the victory over the world is our faith in Christ, not our willpower. Where are you trying to overcome sin or temptation by effort alone, rather than by trusting the Son who has already overcome?
Faith rests on the testimony God has given about His Son, not on our shifting feelings. When your assurance wavers, where do you look first, and how might God's own witness steady you?
John says whoever has the Son has life, and whoever does not have the Son does not have life. How does this sharpen the way you think about people who are religious but not resting in Christ?
God's commandments are not burdensome to those born of God. Which of God's commands still feels like a burden to you, and what would change if love, not duty, drove your obedience?
To be born of God shows itself in loving God's children. Whose face comes to mind as the test of whether that new birth is real in you?

translationNotes.note: 'By water and blood' most likely refers to Jesus' baptism and His atoning death, against teachers who denied that the divine Son truly suffered; the phrase 'not by water alone, but by water and blood' presses that both are real.

A later addition, often called the Johannine Comma, expands these verses in some manuscripts to read 'the Father, the Word, and the Holy Spirit; and these three are one' before 'the Spirit, the water, and the blood'. It stands in the Textus Receptus behind the King James Version but is absent from the earliest Greek manuscripts, so the BSB rightly places it only in a footnote. The doctrine of the Trinity does not depend on it and is taught clearly elsewhere in Scripture.
The BSB renders the clause 'this is the victory that has overcome the world: our faith'; the original marks faith as the decisive means of a victory already won in Christ.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 1 John 5:1-12. Em dashes in the BSB at verses 4, 6, and 8 were rendered with clean punctuation (a colon, a comma, and a semicolon) to match house style; the wording is otherwise unchanged. The Johannine Comma at 5:7-8, absent from the earliest manuscripts and present only as a BSB footnote, is handled in the translation notes.
